author: Jignesh Sheth
artistdescription: Jignesh Sheth is a professional musician, composer, and multi-instrumentalist specializing in tabla and guitar. A disciple of tabla maestros Pt. Anindo Chatterjee and Pt. Divyang Vakil, he is also proficient in mandolin, flute, violin, and keyboard, bringing remarkable versatility and depth to his musical expression. An internationally acclaimed performer, he has collaborated with leading musicians and dancers across India, Europe, and Canada. An ICCR-selected artist, Jignesh served as a tabla teacher and performer at the Jawaharlal Nehru Cultural Centre (Embassy of India, Moscow) and the Moscow State Tchaikovsky Conservatory (2021–2023), actively contributing to the global promotion of Indian music and culture.

projects.title: Shillim Institute/One Landscape Artist Residency : Jignesh Sheth

sections.value: Set within the contemplative landscape of Shillim, the three-week residency unfolded as a profound journey of artistic immersion, sonic inquiry, and personal transformation. The artist entered a space where nature became both companion and collaborator. The surrounding forests, mountain trails, shifting skies, birdsong, and moments of stillness offered not only inspiration but an expanded field of listening. Experiences such as hiking, camping beneath the open sky, village encounters, and the resonant visit to the ancient Bedse Caves deepened the artist’s sensitivity to sound, silence, and spatial vibration. Sound therapy and Vedanta sessions further enriched this process, opening pathways into the healing and philosophical dimensions of music. In this rare environment of creative freedom, every shared meal, conversation, and solitary reflection became part of an unfolding artistic practice—one that reawakened the artist’s relationship with music as both inner discipline and living presence.

Collaboration and performance formed the vibrant heart of the Shillim residency, allowing the artist to expand musical language through dialogue across traditions, forms, and communities. One of the most compelling aspects of the residency was the opportunity to work with fellow artists from diverse disciplines and backgrounds. The artist experimented with tabla accompaniment for Odissi dance with Shatabdi, creating a nuanced conversation between rhythm and classical movement. Equally exciting was the collaboration with contemporary dancers Constantine and Rebecca, where tabla was explored in an innovative and fluid context, building a bridge between Indian classical rhythm structures and international contemporary movement practices. This exchange opened new possibilities of interconnection between rooted classical forms and global contemporary expression. The artist also engaged in impromptu jam sessions with local folk musicians from the village, blending guitar and dholak to create a distinctive fusion of traditions shaped by spontaneity and cultural exchange. These interactions with village musicians, along with a visit to a folk musician’s home, offered a deeper understanding of regional musical heritage and enriched the artist’s evolving sonic vocabulary. Performance became another significant extension of this creative journey. At the memorable Dharohar concert held at Ojas, Shillim, on 10th February, the artist shared the stage with accomplished Indian classical musicians from Pune, receiving an enthusiastic and heartfelt response from the audience. In Pune, the artist presented diverse performances including a traditional tabla solo, an original guitar composition titled Joy accompanied by folk musicians on dholak, and an experimental presentation of a self-composed Ram bhajan performed on tabla alongside Odissi dance. These performances reflected versatility, risk-taking, and a sincere commitment to expanding the expressive possibilities of Indian music through collaboration, improvisation, and interdisciplinary artistic dialogue.
